papers.title: The VLT-FLAMES Tarantula Survey: Observational evidence for two distinct populations of massive runaway stars in 30 Doradus
papers.author_names: H. Sana O. H. Ramírez-Agudelo V. Hénault-Brunet L. Mahy L. A. Almeida A. de Koter J. M. Bestenlehner C. J. Evans N. Langer F. R. N. Schneider P. A. Crowther S. E. de Mink A. Herrero D. J. Lennon M. Gieles J. Maíiz Apellániz M. Renzo E. Sabbi J. Th. van Loon J. S. Vink

abstract.text: Two main scenarios have been proposed for origin of massive runaway stars -- dynamical ejection or release from a binary at the first core collapse -- but their relative contribution remains debated.\\\n  Using two large spectroscopic campaigns towards massive stars in 30 Doradus, we aim to provide observational constraints on the properties of the O-type runaway population in the most massive active star-forming region in the Local group.\\\n  We use RV measurements of the O-type star populations in 30 Doradus obtained by the VLT-FLAMES Tarantula Survey and the Tarantula Massive Binary Monitoring to identify single and binary O-type runaways. We discuss their rotational properties and qualitatively compare observations with expectations of ejection scenarios.\\\n  We identify 23 single and one binary O-type runaway objects, most of them outside the main star-forming regions in 30 Doradus. We find an overabundance of rapid rotators (vsini > 200km/s) among the runaway population, providing an explanation of the overabundance of rapidly rotating stars in the 30 Doradus field. Considerations of the projected rotation rates and runaway line-of-sight (los) velocities reveal a conspicuous absence of rapidly rotating (vsini > 210k/ms), fast moving (v_{los} > 60km/s) runaways, and suggest the presence of two different populations of runaway stars: a population of rapidly-spinning but slowly moving runaways and a population of fast moving but slowly rotating ones. These are detected with a ratio close to 2:1 in our sample.\\\n  We argue that slowly moving but rapidly spinning runaways result from binary ejections, while rapidly moving but slowly spinning runaways could result from dynamical ejections. Given that detection biases will more strongly impact the slow-moving population, our results suggest that the binary evolution scenario dominates the current massive runaway population in 30 Doradus.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The VLT-FLAMES Tarantula Survey identifies 23 single and 1 binary O-type runaway stars in 30 Doradus, revealing a 2:1 ratio between two distinct populations: rapidly rotating but slowly moving runaways (likely ejected from binaries) and fast-moving but slowly rotating ones (likely dynamically ejected). The overabundance of rapid rotators supports binary ejection as the dominant mechanism, with detection biases favoring slow-moving stars, implying that binary evolution dominates the current massive runaway population in 30 Doradus.

source_fields.title: The VLT-FLAMES Tarantula Survey: Observational evidence for two distinct populations of massive runaway stars in 30 Doradus

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper presents the first statistically robust, real-time observational distinction between dynamical and binary explosion mechanisms of massive runaway stars in a young starburst environment by analyzing line-of-sight velocities and projected spins of O-type stars in the 30 Doradus region, resolving long-standing debates on their formation efficiency and ejection rates. Observational data: The VLT-FLAMES Tarantula Survey and its follow-up monitoring program provide comprehensive spectroscopic data on O-type stars in the 30 Doradus region, enabling the identification and detailed characterization of massive spectroscopic binaries through radial velocity measurements and orbital parameter determination. Discussion and conclusions: The 30 Doradus runaway population shows a significant overabundance of rapidly rotating stars (55% with $v_{\\mathrm{e}}\\sin i > 200$ km s⁻¹) compared to non-runaways (25%), indicating a predominance of binary interaction origins over dynamical ejection, with at least three massive (30–50 M☉) post-binary interaction runaways suggesting natal kicks are necessary for the ejection of massive stellar companions. Appendix A Gaia DR3 proper motions: The Gaia DR3 proper motion analysis of VFTS O stars shows that the runaway desert remains underpopulated in velocity space, with limited detection of high-velocity objects due to measurement uncertainties and crowding effects, despite providing a preliminary challenge to the traditional zone of avoidance. Rotational properties of runaway stars: Runaway stars exhibit distinct rotational and kinematic properties, with a significant overabundance of rapidly spinning, slowly moving stars and a "runaway desert" lacking rapidly spinning fast-moving stars, indicating a clear separation between runaway populations. Identifying runaway stars through their peculiar line-of-sight velocity: Runaway stars in the 30 Dor region are identified by their line-of-sight velocities deviating by more than 3σ (25.8 km s⁻¹) from the systemic velocity of the NGC 2070 O-star population, yielding a runaway fraction of 7±1%, with one binary candidate (VFTS 661) near the threshold.

title: VLT-FLAMES Tarantula Survey: Observational evidence for two distinct populations of massive runaway stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper identifies 23 single and 1 binary O-type runaway stars using RV measurements from VLT-FLAMES Tarantula Survey, presents observational data on velocities and rotation rates.

papers.title: Stellar Escape from Globular Clusters. I. Escape Mechanisms and Properties at Ejection
papers.author_names: Newlin C. Weatherford Fulya Kiroglu Giacomo Fragione Sourav Chatterjee Kyle Kremer Frederic A. Rasio

abstract.text: The theory of stellar escape from globular clusters (GCs) dates back nearly a century, especially the gradual evaporation of GCs via two-body relaxation coupled with external tides. More violent ejection can also occur via strong gravitational scattering, supernovae, gravitational wave-driven mergers, tidal disruption events, and physical collisions, but comprehensive study of the many escape mechanisms has been limited. Recent exquisite kinematic data from the Gaia space telescope has revealed numerous stellar streams in the Milky Way (MW) and traced the origin of many to specific MWGCs, highlighting the need for further examination of stellar escape from these clusters. In this study, the first of a series, we lay the groundwork for detailed follow-up comparisons between Cluster Monte Carlo (CMC) GC models and the latest Gaia data on the outskirts of MWGCs, their tidal tails, and associated streams. We thoroughly review escape mechanisms from GCs and examine their relative contributions to the escape rate, ejection velocities, and escaper demographics. We show for the first time that three-body binary formation may dominate high-speed ejection from typical MWGCs, potentially explaining some of the hypervelocity stars in the MW. Due to their mass, black holes strongly catalyze this process, and their loss at the onset of observable core collapse, characterized by a steep central brightness profile, dramatically curtails three-body binary formation, despite the increased post-collapse density. We also demonstrate that even when born from a thermal eccentricity distribution, escaping binaries have significantly nonthermal eccentricities consistent with the roughly uniform distribution observed in the Galactic field.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: stellar escaper candidates, stellar interactions in dense systems LLM confirmed: Directly studies stellar escape/ejection mechanisms from globular clusters, explicitly mentions hypervelocity stars and ejection velocities

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Results: Escapers from NCC'd and CC'd globular clusters exhibit distinct escape mechanisms, with escape velocities typically below the local escape speed and positions concentrated near the tidal radius, where the fraction of escapers is higher in more tidally filled clusters due to lower escape speeds and faster dynamical evolution. Footnotes: The gravothermal catastrophe prevents thermal equilibrium in gravitational systems, and early binary ejections in core-collapsed globular clusters are likely due to initial binary properties influenced by a linear scaling of semi-major axis with radius, affecting mass transfer and stability. Discussion: Three-body binary formation significantly drives high-speed stellar ejections in Milky Way-like globular clusters before and during core collapse, with ejection rates and binary burning dynamics heavily influenced by black hole presence and encounter hardness, challenging long-held assumptions that 3BBF is negligible prior to core collapse. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel theoretical framework that accurately models stellar escape from globular clusters under realistic tidal and dynamical conditions, addressing the long-standing challenge of predicting and comparing escaper kinematics with Gaia observations to improve Galactic archaeology and constrain key cluster properties. Cluster Models: The paper proposes a detailed Monte Carlo simulation approach using the CMC code to model the evolution of massive globular clusters, focusing on how initial virial radius and galactocentric distance influence dynamical processes like core collapse and central black hole ejection through realistic physics including stellar evolution, two-body relaxation, and binary interactions. Escape Mechanisms: This section reviews escape mechanisms from star clusters, distinguishing between sudden ejection events (like gravitational encounters or supernovae) and gradual evaporation processes (such as two-body relaxation and tidal stripping), emphasizing that evaporation mechanisms, despite being less diverse, dominate the overall escape rate in realistic globular clusters. Summary And Future Work: This study identifies key escape mechanisms from globular clusters, highlighting the dominant role of three-body binary formation driven by black holes and the importance of future work comparing modeled ejecta to Gaia observations to understand stellar stream formation and constrain globular cluster properties.

abstract: We show for the first time that three-body binary formation may dominate high-speed ejection from typical MWGCs, potentially explaining some of the hypervelocity stars in the MW.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Theoretical/simulation paper reviewing escape mechanisms from globular clusters using CMC models. Mentions Gaia data for motivation but focuses on mechanisms, ejection velocities, and demographics from simulations.
